cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The power of a court to determine whether a law or executive action violates the Constitution and to declare it invalid; established in Marbury v. Madison (1803)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A directive issued by the president that has the force of law without congressional approval, used to manage operations of the executive branch

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The presidential power to reject a bill passed by Congress, preventing it from becoming law; Congress can override this with a two-thirds vote in both chambers

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A presidential action taken when Congress is in session in which the president withholds signature on a bill for 10 days, causing it to die without becoming law

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A Senate procedural tactic of extending debate indefinitely to delay or block a vote on legislation; requires 60 votes to end (cloture)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The Senate vote (requiring 60 senators) to end debate and force a vote on a bill or nomination, often used to break a filibuster

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The manipulation of district boundaries to favor one political party or group; can be partisan or racial in nature

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The constitutionally mandated Senate role of approving presidential nominations for judges, ambassadors, and senior executive officials, as well as ratifying treaties

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The process by which the House of Representatives formally charges a president, vice president, or federal judge with 'high crimes and misdemeanors'; does not remove from office

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The claim by presidents that communications between the president and advisors are confidential and protected from congressional or judicial compulsion

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A written statement the president issues when signing legislation, indicating how the executive branch intends to interpret and enforce the law

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The complex system of agencies, departments, commissions, and government corporations that implement federal law and policy; the 'fourth branch' of government

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A stable, mutually beneficial policy alliance among a congressional committee, an executive agency, and a relevant interest group that together dominate policymaking in a narrow area

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A looser, less stable set of relationships among various actors (advocates, journalists, researchers, officials) who share an interest in a policy area but lack the tight reciprocal benefits of the iron triangle

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The authority granted to executive agencies to make decisions based on their own judgment within the broad guidelines set by law

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The power of executive agencies to create rules that have the force of law, subject to the notice-and-comment process under the Administrative Procedure Act

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Federal agencies (such as the FCC or SEC) that are insulated from direct presidential removal and operate with some independence to regulate specific industries

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The tradition in which senators from a president's party are consulted and given deference on judicial and executive appointments within their state

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A temporary committee formed when the House and Senate pass different versions of a bill; members from both chambers negotiate a compromise version

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Permanent legislative committees with jurisdiction over specific policy areas; the most powerful committees in Congress, where most substantive legislative work occurs

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The congressional norm granting the most senior member of the majority party automatic chairmanship of committees, regardless of competence

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Legislation that funds specific projects in a legislator's district primarily for political benefit rather than national need; also called 'earmarks'

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The practice of legislators trading votes — agreeing to support each other's bills in exchange for reciprocal support

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A procedure allowing a majority of House members to bypass committee review and bring a bill directly to the floor for a vote; requires signatures of 218 members

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The 1973 law requiring presidents to notify Congress within 48 hours of deploying troops and limiting military engagement to 60 days without congressional authorization

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Theodore Roosevelt's term describing the presidency as a platform for advocating policy — the president uses the office's visibility to shape public opinion and pressure Congress

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Presidential strategy of appealing directly to the public through speeches, media appearances, and social media to build support for policy proposals, bypassing Congress

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Latin for 'let the decision stand'; the principle that courts follow precedent in deciding cases, promoting consistency and predictability in the law

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A formal order issued by the Supreme Court to a lower court directing it to send up the case record for review; the Court's mechanism for selecting its cases

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A legal brief filed by a non-party with an interest in the outcome of a case; literally 'friend of the court'; common tool for interest groups to influence Supreme Court decisions

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The Supreme Court opinion that settles the case and has the force of law; represents the reasoning endorsed by a majority of justices

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A Supreme Court opinion written by a justice who disagrees with the majority decision; has no legal force but can influence future cases and scholarly debate

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The redistribution of House seats among states based on population changes recorded by the decennial census

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Congressional oversight mechanism in which committees hold formal hearings to review agency activities, gather testimony, and investigate possible misconduct

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The process by which both chambers of Congress approve proposed constitutional amendments by two-thirds vote, then three-fourths of states ratify them

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The 4th Chief Justice of the United States who authored Marbury v. Madison (1803) establishing judicial review; served 34 years and shaped American constitutional law more than any other jurist

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Chief Justice who presided over the Warren Court (1953–1969); authored Brown v. Board of Education and oversaw landmark expansions of civil rights and civil liberties

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The first African American Supreme Court Justice (1967–1991); argued and won Brown v. Board of Education as an NAACP attorney before his appointment to the Court

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The first female Supreme Court Justice (1981–2006), appointed by President Reagan; her swing-vote position on the Rehnquist Court made her especially influential on key 5-4 decisions

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: President who significantly expanded executive power through the New Deal; attempted to 'pack' the Supreme Court by adding six new justices when the Court struck down New Deal legislation

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: President who issued Executive Order 9981 desegregating the armed forces (1948) and faced a constitutional crisis when he attempted to seize steel mills during the Korean War

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: President whose resignation after the Watergate scandal was preceded by Supreme Court orders to release executive-privilege-protected tapes; established limits on presidential secrecy

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Established judicial review — the Supreme Court's power to strike down laws that violate the Constitution; arose when Chief Justice Marshall ruled part of the Judiciary Act of 1789 unconstitutional

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Ruled that federal courts can hear challenges to state legislative redistricting plans; established that redistricting is a justiciable political question, paving the way for 'one person, one vote'

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Ruled that race cannot be the predominant factor in drawing congressional district lines; challenged a North Carolina district specifically designed to create a majority-minority district

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Struck down the federal Gun-Free School Zones Act as exceeding Congress's commerce power; first time in 60 years the Court limited congressional authority under the Commerce Clause

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Ruled that prior restraint on publication of the Pentagon Papers was unconstitutional; established a strong presumption against government censorship of the press
cards.answer: New York Times Co. v. United States

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Ruled that political spending by corporations and unions is protected free speech; struck down limits on independent expenditures in elections, fundamentally reshaping campaign finance
cards.answer: Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: According to Federalist No. 78, Hamilton argued that the judiciary is the 'least dangerous branch' primarily because it

choices.text: Has neither the power of the purse (held by Congress) nor the sword (held by the executive) and must rely on persuasion
choices.explanation: Hamilton argued the judiciary controls neither funds nor force — it must rely on the force of reasoned argument and the legitimacy of its judgments to be effective.

choices.text: Is directly elected by the people and therefore most accountable to popular will
choices.explanation: Federal judges are appointed, not elected — Hamilton's argument for judicial safety was precisely that judges are insulated from popular pressure, not accountable to it.

choices.text: Can be overruled by the president through executive orders on any constitutional question
choices.explanation: Presidents cannot override Supreme Court constitutional interpretations through executive orders — the Court's judgments on constitutional questions are final within the system.

choices.text: Has the smallest budget of any branch and therefore poses no fiscal threat
choices.explanation: Hamilton's 'least dangerous branch' argument was about constitutional power structure, not budget size — budget is irrelevant to his reasoning.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A member of Congress supports a bill funding a new dam in a colleague's district in exchange for that colleague's vote on a farm subsidy bill. This best illustrates which congressional practice?

choices.text: Logrolling
choices.explanation: Logrolling is the practice of trading votes — each legislator agrees to support the other's preferred legislation in exchange for reciprocal support.

choices.text: Gerrymandering
choices.explanation: Gerrymandering refers to drawing district boundaries to favor one party — it has no connection to vote trading between legislators.

choices.text: Discharge petition
choices.explanation: A discharge petition allows a majority of House members to force a bill out of committee — it is a procedural mechanism, not a vote-trading arrangement.

choices.text: Filibustering
choices.explanation: Filibustering is a Senate tactic to delay a vote by extending debate — it is a blocking strategy, not a vote-trading arrangement.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) issues a regulation requiring power plants to reduce emissions, relying on authority granted by the Clean Air Act. This action most directly illustrates which concept?

choices.text: Rule-making authority — agencies exercise delegated legislative power by creating specific regulations under broad statutory mandates
choices.explanation: Congress passes broad laws like the Clean Air Act and delegates rule-making authority to agencies like the EPA to create specific enforceable standards — a key feature of the modern administrative state.

choices.text: Judicial review — the EPA reviews the constitutionality of power plant practices
choices.explanation: Judicial review is a court power to assess constitutionality — agencies enforce and implement law but do not perform judicial review of legislation.

choices.text: Separation of powers — the executive branch checks the legislative branch's excessive regulation
choices.explanation: The EPA is acting under congressional authority here, not checking it — this is implementation, not a check on Congress.

choices.text: Executive privilege — the agency protects confidential communications from congressional oversight
choices.explanation: Executive privilege involves protecting presidential communications from disclosure — it has no connection to agency rule-making on emissions standards.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best describes a key difference between the House Rules Committee and the Senate filibuster as procedural tools?

choices.text: The Rules Committee enables the majority to control the terms of debate; the filibuster empowers the minority to block votes indefinitely
choices.explanation: The House Rules Committee (controlled by the majority) determines debate time and amendment rules; the Senate filibuster (requiring 60 votes to end) gives a substantial minority blocking power.

choices.text: The Rules Committee is used only in the Senate; the filibuster is used only in the House
choices.explanation: This is backwards — the Rules Committee is a House institution; the filibuster is a Senate practice. The House has strict time limits that prevent filibusters.

choices.text: Both mechanisms require a two-thirds supermajority vote to invoke
choices.explanation: Neither requires two-thirds — the Rules Committee acts by simple majority; cloture to end a filibuster requires three-fifths (60 senators), not two-thirds.

choices.text: The filibuster speeds up legislative action; the Rules Committee slows it down
choices.explanation: The filibuster is a delaying/blocking tactic; the Rules Committee can either expedite or restrict floor action depending on how it structures debate.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A defense contractor lobbies the Armed Services Committee for higher weapons procurement budgets; the committee members secure funding in the defense appropriations bill; the Department of Defense then awards contracts to the same company. This scenario most directly illustrates which concept?

choices.text: Iron triangle — a mutually reinforcing relationship among an interest group, a congressional committee, and a government agency
choices.explanation: The classic iron triangle: the defense contractor (interest group) funds legislators and employs former officials; the Armed Services Committee (Congress) appropriates funds; the DoD (agency) contracts with the company — each benefits at public expense.

choices.text: Issue network — a broad coalition of policy advocates who share information about defense policy
choices.explanation: Issue networks are broader, less structured relationships — the tight three-way mutually reinforcing relationship with direct financial benefits described here is the defining feature of an iron triangle, not an issue network.

choices.text: Senatorial courtesy — Senate deference to home-state senators on executive appointments
choices.explanation: Senatorial courtesy applies to the confirmation of presidential nominees, not to the funding/contracting relationships described in this scenario.

choices.text: Logrolling — committee members trading votes on defense and non-defense bills
choices.explanation: Logrolling is a vote-trading arrangement between legislators — the iron triangle specifically involves an outside interest group and an executive agency, not just congressional vote trading.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Congress passes legislation cutting the budget of an executive agency after determining the agency exceeded its statutory authority. This most directly illustrates which constitutional mechanism?

choices.text: Legislative oversight — Congress using its power of the purse to hold the bureaucracy accountable
choices.explanation: Congressional oversight includes budget control — cutting agency funding is a tool Congress uses to check bureaucratic behavior and align agency actions with legislative intent.

choices.text: Executive privilege — the president protecting agency communications from congressional scrutiny
choices.explanation: Executive privilege protects presidential communications — it is a shield against disclosure, not a legislative action to cut budgets.

choices.text: Judicial review — federal courts striking down the agency's rule as unconstitutional
choices.explanation: Judicial review is a court power — Congress cutting an agency's budget is a legislative action, not a judicial one.

choices.text: Signing statements — the president issuing guidance on how to implement the budget cut
choices.explanation: Signing statements interpret legislation at the time of signing — they are a presidential action, not a congressional oversight mechanism.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The Senate confirms a presidential nomination to the Supreme Court by a 52–48 vote. This process most directly illustrates which constitutional principle?

choices.text: Checks and balances — the Senate uses its advice and consent power to check presidential appointments
choices.explanation: The Senate's confirmation power over judicial appointments is a classic checks-and-balances mechanism — the executive proposes but the legislature must approve.

choices.text: Separation of powers — the judicial branch independently selecting its own members
choices.explanation: If the judiciary selected its own members, it would be self-perpetuating — the confirmation process is specifically designed to involve two branches, not the judiciary selecting itself.

choices.text: Popular sovereignty — the people directly choosing who serves on the Supreme Court
choices.explanation: Federal judges are appointed, not elected — the confirmation process involves elected officials but is not a direct expression of popular sovereignty.

choices.text: Federalism — states having a role in appointing federal judges
choices.explanation: Federalism concerns the division of power between national and state governments — the confirmation of federal judges involves only federal actors (president and Senate).

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Presidents have increasingly used executive orders to achieve policy goals that Congress has refused to enact. Critics argue this practice violates separation of powers. Defenders argue it is justified because

choices.text: The executive power vested in the president by Article II includes broad authority to manage the executive branch and direct its operations
choices.explanation: Defenders cite Article II's vesting clause and the president's role as commander-in-chief and chief executive as grounds for orders directing how federal agencies implement existing law.

choices.text: Executive orders are equivalent to constitutional amendments and therefore override congressional action
choices.explanation: Executive orders have far less legal weight than constitutional amendments — they can be reversed by future presidents and struck down by courts, unlike constitutional amendments.

choices.text: The Necessary and Proper Clause grants the executive unlimited lawmaking authority during national emergencies
choices.explanation: The Necessary and Proper Clause is a congressional (legislative) power in Article I, not a presidential power — it does not grant executive lawmaking authority.

choices.text: The Supreme Court has unanimously upheld all presidential executive orders as constitutional
choices.explanation: Courts have struck down executive orders before — for example, Truman's steel seizure was invalidated in Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer — so this claim is factually false.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why the Supreme Court agreed to hear only about 1–2% of the cases petitioned to it each year?

choices.text: The 'Rule of Four' requires only four justices to agree to grant certiorari, allowing the Court to selectively focus on cases involving unsettled constitutional questions or circuit splits
choices.explanation: The Court uses certiorari to focus on cases with national importance — circuit splits, unresolved constitutional questions, and policy implications — rather than serving as a general appellate court.

choices.text: The Constitution limits the Court to hearing only cases involving the federal government as a party
choices.explanation: The Court hears many cases not involving the federal government as a party — its jurisdiction includes federal questions and diversity cases under Article III.

choices.text: The Court must first ask Congress for permission before accepting each case for review
choices.explanation: The Court's certiorari decisions are independent — it does not seek congressional approval. Its jurisdiction is defined by statute and the Constitution, but case selection is internal.

choices.text: Lower courts are not permitted to appeal to the Supreme Court without authorization from the president
choices.explanation: Parties (not lower courts) petition for certiorari and no presidential approval is needed — the Court selects cases based on their own internal deliberations.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following most accurately describes the role of the House Rules Committee in the legislative process?

choices.text: It sets the conditions under which bills are debated on the House floor, including time limits, amendment rules, and the order of consideration
choices.explanation: The Rules Committee is the 'traffic cop' of the House — it issues special rules for each major bill specifying how long it can be debated and whether amendments are allowed.

choices.text: It reviews all presidential nominations and recommends whether the full Senate should confirm them
choices.explanation: Presidential nominations are reviewed by Senate committees — the House Rules Committee is a House institution with no role in Senate confirmation proceedings.

choices.text: It drafts new legislation on behalf of the majority party and introduces bills directly to the floor
choices.explanation: The Rules Committee controls floor procedures — it does not draft legislation. Bills are drafted by members or their staff and referred to substantive committees.

choices.text: It reviews enacted laws to determine whether they comply with the Constitution before the president can sign them
choices.explanation: Constitutional review is performed by courts (judicial review) — not by the Rules Committee, which deals with procedural rules for floor debate.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following presidential actions would most likely be interpreted as an example of 'going public'?

choices.text: Delivering a prime-time televised address to urge citizens to contact their senators in support of a pending tax reform bill
choices.explanation: Going public is precisely this strategy — using mass communication to mobilize public pressure on Congress rather than negotiating privately with legislative leaders.

choices.text: Issuing an executive order requiring federal agencies to reduce regulatory paperwork
choices.explanation: Executive orders bypass Congress entirely — going public is specifically about using public pressure to influence congressional action, not circumventing the legislature.

choices.text: Quietly negotiating with Senate leadership over the terms of a bipartisan compromise bill
choices.explanation: Private negotiation with congressional leaders is the traditional 'inside game' — going public is the opposite approach, using public opinion as leverage.

choices.text: Nominating a well-known former senator to serve as Secretary of State
choices.explanation: Nominations are a constitutional appointment power — making a high-profile nomination is not an example of going public to build legislative support.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Members of Congress regularly vote in ways that benefit their districts rather than the national interest, sometimes contradicting their party leadership. This behavior is best explained by which of the following?

choices.text: The electoral incentive — members prioritize winning re-election, which depends on satisfying local constituents rather than following party or national priorities
choices.explanation: David Mayhew's influential argument: legislators are primarily motivated by re-election, which creates constant pressure to deliver district benefits even at the expense of national policy coherence.

choices.text: The iron triangle — bureaucratic agencies force members to vote against party lines
choices.explanation: Iron triangles involve policy alliances — they don't force individual legislative votes. The district-serving behavior described is an electoral motivation, not bureaucratic pressure.

choices.text: The seniority rule — senior members use their committee chairmanships to override junior members' district interests
choices.explanation: The seniority rule determines committee leadership — it does not cause members to vote against the national interest in favor of their districts.

choices.text: Logrolling — members always vote their conscience regardless of electoral pressure
choices.explanation: Logrolling describes vote trading between members — it is a deal-making strategy, not an explanation for why members vote their district's interests over national interests.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best describes the constitutional basis for congressional oversight of executive agencies?

choices.text: Congressional oversight is implied by Congress's power to legislate, appropriate funds, and confirm appointments — it must have authority to ensure laws are faithfully executed
choices.explanation: The Constitution does not explicitly grant oversight power, but it is considered an implied power essential to Congress's ability to legislate and appropriate — without oversight, Congress cannot ensure its laws are implemented.

choices.text: The Constitution explicitly grants Congress the power to investigate executive agencies in Article I
choices.explanation: Congressional oversight is not explicitly mentioned in the Constitution — it developed through practice and is considered an implied rather than enumerated power.

choices.text: Oversight power was granted to Congress by a Supreme Court ruling establishing it as a constitutional right
choices.explanation: While courts have supported congressional oversight, it developed from legislative practice, not from a single Supreme Court ruling granting it as a right.

choices.text: Congressional oversight is limited by executive privilege, which prevents any investigation of the executive branch
choices.explanation: Executive privilege is not absolute — it can sometimes be overridden by compelling congressional need, and does not prevent oversight generally. United States v. Nixon established its limits.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A gerrymandered district creates a safe seat for the incumbent party, resulting in a representative who rarely faces competitive general elections. Which of the following is the most likely consequence for representation?

choices.text: The representative is more likely to respond to primary voters with strong ideological preferences, potentially making moderation and compromise less politically rewarding
choices.explanation: In a safe seat, the real electoral threat comes from a primary challenger from the base — this incentivizes ideological purity over bipartisan compromise, contributing to political polarization.

choices.text: The representative is more likely to vote with the opposing party to appeal to general election swing voters
choices.explanation: In a safe seat, there are few swing voters in the general election — the incentive is to satisfy primary voters (the ideological base), not to appeal across party lines.

choices.text: Gerrymandering results in equal representation for all demographic groups within the district
choices.explanation: Gerrymandering is specifically designed to advantage one group or party — it typically distorts rather than equalizes representation.

choices.text: The district's representative has more power in the House because safe seats lead to greater seniority
choices.explanation: While safe seats may help members accumulate seniority, that is an incidental effect — the more significant representation consequence is the ideological extremism incentive described in choice A.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why the Senate confirmation process for Supreme Court justices has become increasingly contentious in recent decades?

choices.text: The Supreme Court's expanded role in resolving major social and political issues makes its composition highly consequential, increasing the political stakes of each confirmation
choices.explanation: As the Court has decided landmark cases on abortion, gun rights, health care, and elections, party leaders have increasingly viewed confirmation battles as proxy wars over policy, making them far more contentious.

choices.text: The Constitution was recently amended to require a two-thirds Senate supermajority to confirm justices
choices.explanation: No such amendment exists — Supreme Court confirmation requires only a simple majority of senators present and voting, and the filibuster for nominations was eliminated in 2017.

choices.text: The president now shares nomination power with the House of Representatives
choices.explanation: The House plays no formal role in confirming federal judges — the power of advice and consent belongs exclusively to the Senate under Article II.

choices.text: The Supreme Court lost public legitimacy after several justices were impeached for ethical violations
choices.explanation: No Supreme Court justice has been removed by impeachment — while ethics concerns have arisen, this is not the reason for increasingly contentious confirmations.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: In which of the following ways does the president's role as commander in chief create tension with Congress's war powers?

choices.text: Presidents have deployed troops into combat without a formal declaration of war from Congress, while Congress has rarely used the War Powers Resolution to force troop withdrawal
choices.explanation: Since WWII, presidents have used military force in Korea, Vietnam, Iraq, Afghanistan, and elsewhere without formal declarations of war — and Congress has never successfully invoked the War Powers Resolution's 60-day clock against a president.

choices.text: Congress frequently declares war while presidents consistently refuse to deploy troops, creating a deadlock
choices.explanation: The opposite tension exists — presidents deploy troops often; Congress has not formally declared war since 1942. Congress has not been more aggressive in using war powers than the president.

choices.text: The Supreme Court has ruled that only Congress can authorize any use of military force
choices.explanation: The Court has generally avoided ruling on the war powers dispute between Congress and the president, treating it as a political question — there is no ruling giving Congress exclusive authority.

choices.text: The War Powers Resolution has been consistently enforced through judicial review, limiting presidential military actions
choices.explanation: Courts have largely declined to enforce the War Powers Resolution, and no president has acknowledged its constitutionality — judicial review has not been the enforcement mechanism here.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A federal appellate court in one region rules that a law is constitutional, while another appellate court in a different region rules the same law is unconstitutional. This situation most directly creates a need for

choices.text: Supreme Court review via certiorari, to resolve the circuit split and establish uniform national interpretation
choices.explanation: A circuit split — where different federal appellate courts reach opposite conclusions on the same legal question — is one of the primary factors the Supreme Court considers when granting certiorari.

choices.text: Congressional legislation to override both court decisions and clarify the law's meaning
choices.explanation: While Congress can respond to judicial decisions with new legislation, the direct mechanism for resolving conflicting circuit interpretations is Supreme Court review, not new legislation.

choices.text: Presidential executive order to direct federal agencies to follow one circuit's ruling nationwide
choices.explanation: Presidents cannot override judicial decisions through executive orders — only the Supreme Court can establish a binding uniform interpretation of federal law.

choices.text: A constitutional amendment to specify which circuit's ruling takes precedence
choices.explanation: Constitutional amendments are not used to resolve routine circuit splits — that is exactly the Supreme Court's function under its certiorari jurisdiction.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best illustrates the principal-agent problem in the context of bureaucratic control?

choices.text: A regulatory agency prioritizes its own institutional interests and the preferences of the industries it regulates rather than the objectives of the Congress that created it
choices.explanation: The principal-agent problem occurs when agents (agencies/bureaucrats) pursue their own interests or those of organized constituencies rather than the goals of their principals (Congress/the president) who lack complete information to monitor them.

choices.text: Congress passes a law that creates a new agency to implement a policy the president opposes
choices.explanation: This describes inter-branch conflict between principal (Congress) and the executive — not the principal-agent problem, which specifically concerns divergence between the agency (agent) and its legislative/executive principals.

choices.text: The Supreme Court rules that a congressional statute is unconstitutional
choices.explanation: Judicial review is a constitutional check, not a principal-agent problem — the issue is about agents (agencies) diverging from principal (legislative/executive) goals.

choices.text: States refuse to comply with a federal mandate, forcing Congress to pass new enforcement legislation
choices.explanation: State non-compliance with federal mandates is a federalism issue — the principal-agent problem specifically concerns bureaucratic drift from legislative intent within the federal executive branch.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The president signs a major climate bill into law but attaches a signing statement indicating the administration will not enforce sections it considers unconstitutional infringements on executive authority. This action most directly raises concerns about

choices.text: Whether the executive can selectively enforce laws, effectively giving the president a line-item veto that was not granted by the Constitution
choices.explanation: Signing statements that assert non-enforcement authority effectively allow presidents to nullify specific provisions — critics argue this usurps the legislative power and violates the president's duty to 'faithfully execute' the laws.

choices.text: Whether Congress has the authority to investigate executive signing statements through subpoenas
choices.explanation: While oversight is relevant, the primary constitutional concern raised by non-enforcement signing statements is whether the executive is improperly nullifying legislative enactments — not about congressional subpoena authority.

choices.text: Whether the Supreme Court must automatically review the constitutionality of any signing statement
choices.explanation: Signing statements are not automatically reviewed by the Court — judicial review requires a party with standing to challenge the relevant enforcement action in court.

choices.text: Whether federal agencies are permitted to implement policies that conflict with state environmental laws
choices.explanation: Federal-state environmental conflicts are a federalism issue — the signing statement concerns internal separation of powers between the executive and legislative branches of the federal government.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Hamilton's argument in Federalist No. 70 that the executive should be 'energetic' with a single person at its head most directly supported which feature of the Constitution?

choices.text: A single unitary executive — one president who alone holds executive authority, ensuring clear accountability and decisiveness
choices.explanation: Hamilton explicitly argued against a multi-member executive — a single president creates clear responsibility, quick decision-making, and accountability that a council or committee executive would undermine.

choices.text: A two-term limit on the presidency to prevent tyranny through repeated re-election
choices.explanation: Term limits were not part of Hamilton's Federalist 70 argument — they were addressed by constitutional amendment (22nd Amendment in 1951), not by the original framers in Hamilton's essay.

choices.text: Congressional control over the president's cabinet appointments to prevent executive overreach
choices.explanation: Hamilton's Federalist 70 argues for executive energy and strength — not for legislative control that would weaken the executive he sought to empower.

choices.text: The establishment of an independent judiciary to check executive power
choices.explanation: An independent judiciary checking the executive is the subject of Federalist 78, not Federalist 70 — each Federalist paper has a distinct focus.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best describes how stare decisis affects Supreme Court decision-making?

choices.text: It creates a presumption that prior rulings should be followed, providing legal stability and predictability, though the Court can overturn precedent when circumstances justify it
choices.explanation: Stare decisis is a principle, not an absolute rule — the Court follows it unless it determines that prior precedent was wrongly decided or circumstances have changed fundamentally, as it did when overturning Plessy in Brown.

choices.text: It requires the Court to defer to congressional legislation rather than overturning any prior congressional act
choices.explanation: Stare decisis applies to the Court's own prior precedents, not to congressional deference — judicial review of congressional acts is independent of the stare decisis principle.

choices.text: It mandates that lower federal courts ignore prior Supreme Court decisions that were decided by a 5-4 margin
choices.explanation: A 5-4 majority ruling is still binding precedent — stare decisis applies regardless of the margin by which a decision was reached.

choices.text: It prevents the Supreme Court from ever overturning its own previous decisions
choices.explanation: Stare decisis does not absolutely prevent overruling — the Court has overturned its own precedent (as in Brown overturning Plessy, or Dobbs overturning Roe). It creates a presumption, not an absolute bar.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The Twenty-Second Amendment limiting presidents to two terms is best understood as which type of constitutional check?

choices.text: A structural limit on executive power to prevent the accumulation of political capital that could make a president unaccountable
choices.explanation: The 22nd Amendment was passed after FDR's four terms as a structural safeguard — limiting tenure directly constrains executive power accumulation independent of congressional or judicial action.

choices.text: A judicial check — the Supreme Court limiting presidential terms through constitutional interpretation
choices.explanation: The 22nd Amendment was enacted through the constitutional amendment process (passed by Congress, ratified by states) — it is a constitutional provision, not a judicial ruling.

choices.text: A legislative check — Congress restricting the president's re-election through ordinary legislation
choices.explanation: Ordinary legislation cannot limit presidential terms — it required a constitutional amendment, which is a structural check embedded in the Constitution itself, not a legislative limitation.

choices.text: A federalist check — states collectively limiting the federal executive through the amendment process
choices.explanation: While states ratify amendments, the primary purpose of the 22nd Amendment is limiting executive power accumulation, not a federalist check on federal authority per se.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Citizens United v. FEC (2010) most directly affected the political process by

choices.text: Allowing corporations and unions to spend unlimited amounts on independent political expenditures by extending First Amendment speech protections to these entities
choices.explanation: Citizens United held that the government cannot restrict independent political expenditures by corporations, associations, or labor unions — political spending is a form of protected speech regardless of the spender's identity.

choices.text: Requiring corporations to publicly disclose all campaign contributions above a minimum threshold
choices.explanation: Citizens United struck down expenditure limits — it did not establish new disclosure requirements. Disclosure was a separate issue addressed differently in the decision.

choices.text: Granting individual citizens the right to contribute unlimited amounts directly to candidate campaigns
choices.explanation: Citizens United addressed independent expenditures by organizations, not direct contributions to candidates — contribution limits to campaigns were addressed in different cases.

choices.text: Prohibiting all foreign corporations from participating in U.S. elections
choices.explanation: Citizens United addressed domestic corporations and unions — foreign election spending was addressed separately. The ruling applied to domestic entities' independent expenditures.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following most directly illustrates how the House and Senate differ in their internal procedural rules?

choices.text: The House has strict germaneness requirements and time limits on debate; the Senate allows unlimited debate and the filibuster, giving minorities more power
choices.explanation: The House's size requires strict rules — the Rules Committee limits debate and amendments; the Senate's rules favor deliberation and traditionally allowed minorities to obstruct via filibuster.

choices.text: The Senate uses committee hearings to draft legislation; the House passes all bills without committee review
choices.explanation: Both chambers use standing committees to review legislation before floor consideration — committee review is central to the legislative process in both chambers.

choices.text: The House can ratify treaties; the Senate can originate revenue bills
choices.explanation: These are backwards: the Senate ratifies treaties; the House must originate revenue bills — these are constitutional assignments, not procedural rules.

choices.text: The Senate requires simple majority votes for all legislation; the House allows filibusters on major bills
choices.explanation: The filibuster exists in the Senate, not the House — and the Senate often requires 60 votes for cloture, not just a simple majority, to advance major legislation.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A president nominates a judge known for a narrow view of federal power. Which of the following would most directly represent Congress attempting to check this exercise of judicial appointment power?

choices.text: The Senate Judiciary Committee holding hearings and ultimately voting to recommend rejecting the nomination
choices.explanation: The Senate's advice and consent role is the primary constitutional check on presidential judicial appointments — committee review, hearings, and rejection votes are the mechanisms by which the Senate exercises this check.

choices.text: The House passing a resolution expressing disapproval of the nomination
choices.explanation: The House has no formal role in judicial confirmations — only the Senate can block nominees. House resolutions have no constitutional effect on the confirmation process.

choices.text: The Supreme Court reviewing its own composition and voting to reject the nominee
choices.explanation: The sitting Court has no role in confirming new justices — that authority belongs to the Senate alone.

choices.text: The president issuing a veto of his own nomination to prevent Senate rejection
choices.explanation: Presidents do not veto their own nominations — a president can withdraw a nomination, but the veto applies to legislation, not to executive appointments.
